text.style: font-size: 16px; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; fill: #1A1D23; text-anchor: middle;
text.data-narration: Digraphs are directed graphs with directed edges, each with a direction and a label.

text.style: font-size: 12px; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; fill: #1A1D23; text-anchor: middle;
text.data-narration: A digraph consists of a set of vertices and a set of directed edges, each of which is a directed edge from one vertex to another.

text.style: font-size: 12px; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; fill: #1A1D23; text-anchor: middle;
text.data-narration: Digraphs have a degree sequence, which is a list of the degrees of all the vertices.

text.style: font-size: 12px; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; fill: #1A1D23; text-anchor: middle;
text.data-narration: Digraphs are connected if there is a path from every vertex to every other vertex.

text.style: font-size: 12px; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; fill: #1A1D23; text-anchor: middle;
text.data-narration: Digraphs are strongly connected if there is a path from every vertex to every other vertex in both directions.

text.style: font-size: 12px; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; fill: #1A1D23; text-anchor: middle;
text.data-narration: Binary relations are relations where each pair of vertices is related by a single edge.

text.style: font-size: 12px; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; fill: #1A1D23; text-anchor: middle;
text.data-narration: Binary relations are denoted by a binary relation R, where R is a subset of the Cartesian product of the vertices.

text.style: font-size: 12px; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; fill: #1A1D23; text-anchor: middle;
text.data-narration: Binary relations are reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive.
